metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the updated `Field(description=...)` text for `PlanSeverityAttrs.ac_unverifiable` in the plan (or Scope note for verify.py), so the description matches the new Literal vocabulary and explains the three grades to the LLM.
evidence: verify.py line 259-263: 'ac_unverifiable: str = Field(default="none", description="none|low|medium|high — an acceptance criterion cannot be objectively verified as written. HARD-OVERRIDE axis (any non-none floors impact to 0.85).")' — the description is the live schema instruction.
Plan scope states: 'PlanSeverityAttrs.ac_unverifiable field changes from the ordinal type to Literal[...new grades...]' — specifies the type change but is silent on the Field description text.
The Field description is what structured-output parsers and the LLM output schema surface to the model — it is co-located schema documentation that must match the new enum vocabulary.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `PlanSeverityAttrs.ac_unverifiable` in verify.py changes to `Literal["none", "underspecified_oracle", "broken_oracle", "missing_oracle"]`, but does not specify the updated Pydantic `Field(description=...)` text for that field. The current description ('none|low|medium|high — an acceptance criterion cannot be objectively verified as written. HARD-OVERRIDE axis (any non-none floors impact to 0.85).') is the text the LLM reads from the structured output schema; if it is left unchanged while the type becomes a Literal, the LLM is given contradictory guidance (description says old ordinals, type enforces new grades).
scenarios: After the change, the Pydantic type enforces the new vocabulary but the Field description still says 'none|low|medium|high', causing the LLM to attempt old ordinals and then fail Pydantic validation on every new plan-v3 verification — resulting in systematic parse failures until the description is corrected.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Scope/What section to note: the ac_unverifiable Field description changes to: 'none|underspecified_oracle|broken_oracle|missing_oracle — grade the oracle quality: none=no issue; underspecified_oracle=check exists but exact command/value unspecified (advisory only); broken_oracle=stated verification command/path/value is factually wrong; missing_oracle=no verification method exists as phrased. broken_oracle and missing_oracle floor impact to 0.85.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the grading decision rule (when to assign each of missing_oracle / broken_oracle / underspecified_oracle) and at least one worked example per grade to the plan's What section, so the executor has exact prompt text to place in both verifier files rather than inventing it.
evidence: plan_review_verifier.md line 65: 'Grade each none|low|medium|high' and line 68: 'HARD-OVERRIDE axis: any non-none marks the finding auto-high.' — the OLD vocabulary is still the live instruction.
plan_review_verifier_agentic.md line 74: identical old instruction.

Plan spec: 'BOTH verifier prompts define the three grades with one worked example each drawn from the calibration-3 classified sample' — but the plan provides no worked example text or decision-rule wording for the executor to place in the prompts.
Plan spec names the grades but gives no prose for 'when to assign broken_oracle vs underspecified_oracle vs missing_oracle' as a grading rubric the LLM can follow.
findings.finding: Both verifier prompts (plan_review_verifier.md and plan_review_verifier_agentic.md) currently define ac_unverifiable with the OLD grading instruction 'Grade each none|low|medium|high' and rule 'any non-none marks the finding auto-high'. The plan says to add three-grade definitions with one worked example each, but does not specify the verifier decision rule (the instruction text) the LLM should follow to discriminate missing_oracle vs broken_oracle vs underspecified_oracle — leaving an executor to invent the grading criteria. This is a processing protocol that must be co-located with the schema in the prompt.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier.md lines 65-68 and plan_review_verifier_agentic.md lines 71-74; What section / Scope section
scenarios: An executor implementing this plan will invent their own grading rubric text for the three grades, potentially producing a decision boundary that differs from the calibration-3 intent (e.g., conflating underspecified_oracle with broken_oracle), undermining the calibration benefit.
Without a worked example specified in the plan, the two prompts may get inconsistent worked examples from different developers, causing grade drift between single-turn and agentic verifications.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add a subsection 'Verifier prompt wording' with: (a) the replacement instruction text for the ac_unverifiable axis (replacing the current none|low|medium|high line), (b) three bullet definitions with the discriminating criterion for each grade, and (c) the three worked examples drawn from the calibration-3 sample. The plan already has the grade names and percentages — formalise those into actionable prompt copy.

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verifier-prompt note (in both plan_review_verifier.md files) clarifying that `broken_oracle` applies only when the stated verification command/path/value is factually wrong — and that an operator-attested AC whose in-session unverifiability is by design should be graded `none` (not `broken_oracle` or `missing_oracle`), so the enrich fallback is a safety net rather than the primary gate.
evidence: Plan quote: 'broken_oracle is never cleared (a factually wrong stated command is not cured by an attestation)' — the rationale is stated.
Plan quote: 'this file is in Scope and the asymmetry is pinned by test' — the asymmetry is acknowledged.

However: no decision rule is given for how the verifier should be instructed NOT to assign broken_oracle on an operator-attested AC in the first place — the enrich function is a downstream corrective, but if broken_oracle on an OA-AC is not a legitimate grade, the verifier prompts should say so. The plan does not address this.
workflow_ops.py line 115: current guard `if attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none')` — would have cleared broken_oracle too under the old logic.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `enrich_operator_attested`'s guard changes to `in ("missing_oracle", "underspecified_oracle")` so `broken_oracle` is never cleared — but does not specify the decision rule for the edge case where a finding carries `broken_oracle` AND references an operator-attested AC. The current guard would have cleared it; the new guard leaves it set. The consequence (the finding blocks despite the AC being operator-attested) is architecturally asymmetric and surprising, and the plan gives no rationale or routing note for this case.
findings.location: What section: 'OPERATOR-ATTESTED ENRICH' paragraph; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py lines 113-116
scenarios: A verifier grades a finding as broken_oracle for an operator-attested AC (e.g., the stated command is wrong but the AC is OA-tagged). The new enrich guard leaves broken_oracle set, so the finding auto-blocks. But the fact that the AC is operator-attested is arguably a mitigating context. The plan's rationale ('a factually wrong command is not cured by attestation') is sound but should be reflected in the verifier prompt as a grading note, not just in the enrich code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sub-bullet to AC3 (or a new AC) explicitly verifying that `tests/unit/_audit_page_helpers.py` is updated to replace the hardcoded plan-v2 string with plan-v3 equivalents, so the deliverable has a covering criterion.
evidence: Scope lists '_audit_page_helpers.py (hardcoded plan-v2 string updated)' as an in-scope file
No AC mentions _audit_page_helpers.py or verifies that existing helper pins are updated to reflect plan-v3 terminology
findings.finding: The Scope describes updating `tests/unit/_audit_page_helpers.py` (hardcoded plan-v2 string updated) as a concrete deliverable, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this file was changed. If the update is omitted, the existing pin breaks silently without a failing AC.
findings.location: Scope section: 'tests/unit/_audit_page_helpers.py (hardcoded plan-v2 string updated)'; AC1–AC4 (none reference this file)
scenarios: Implementer updates all other test files but skips _audit_page_helpers.py; the helper silently uses stale plan-v2 string; the ACs all pass; downstream test utilities produce misleading fixtures
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 with: '…and the hardcoded plan-v2 string in tests/unit/_audit_page_helpers.py is updated to plan-v3 (verified by the test file diff or a comment asserting the constant).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state explicitly: specify whether plan-v2 readers skip, error on, or coerce plan-v3 sidecar records containing new vocabulary values (underspecified_oracle/broken_oracle/missing_oracle), and confirm that no previously-blocking finding is silently demoted to non-blocking on code rollback.
evidence: 'reverting the change restores plan-v2 semantics and the corpora stay segmented; no expand-contract migration is needed because sidecars are immutable records, not migrated state'
Plan-v2 scoring code uses _SEV01 = {'none','low','medium','high'} — new vocabulary strings ('underspecified_oracle' etc.) are not in this map and would silently default to 0.0 under rolled-back code
T11 requires: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
ABSENCE: the plan does not state what a plan-v2 reader does when it encounters a plan-v3 sidecar with new vocabulary values — whether it skips the record, errors, or silently maps to 0.0
findings.finding: The plan claims rollback is clean ('reverting the change restores plan-v2 semantics') but does not assert the concrete schema STATE of already-written plan-v3 sidecar records under the rolled-back (plan-v2) code. Plan-v3 sidecars written before rollback contain vocabulary values ('underspecified_oracle', 'broken_oracle', 'missing_oracle') that plan-v2 _SEV01 maps do not recognise; those strings would fall through to the .get() default of 0.0, silently scoring genuinely blocking findings as non-blocking after rollback.
findings.location: Scope / ROLLBACK section: 'reverting the change restores plan-v2 semantics and the corpora stay segmented'
scenarios: Code rolls back to plan-v2 after plan-v3 sidecars have been written; plan-v2 scoring reads those sidecars and silently maps 'broken_oracle' or 'missing_oracle' to 0.0, causing real defects to not block reviews
An operator inspects post-rollback scoring output and finds no explanation for why previously-blocking findings are no longer blocking
findings.suggested_fix: Add a forward-compatibility guard in the plan-v2 scoring path (or document it as an existing property): when a sidecar's IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION is 'plan-v3', exclude it from plan-v2 scoring entirely (already implied by 'segmented by version') AND assert in the rollback section that plan-v2 code skips plan-v3 records rather than misscoring them. Alternatively, make the .get() default for an unknown vocabulary string raise a logged warning and return a conservative high score rather than 0.0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1) that runs the verifier end-to-end against at least one representative plan with the live model (or a clearly-labelled integration fixture that exercises the full prompt→parse→score path) and asserts the output grade falls within {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} and that the resulting impact score is within the expected range.
evidence: All four ACs are pinned exclusively by unit tests and doc checks; none invokes the verifier pipeline against a real or representative plan with the live model.
'Pydantic strict validation rejects out-of-vocabulary grades at verification-parse time' — the gate catches bad LLM output, but no AC asserts the LLM actually emits valid output with the new prompts.

Anti-FP: '(f) is not-applicable when the cutover stays behind a non-default opt-in flag' — plan-v3 IS the new default for all new verifications after the version bump, so the exemption does not apply.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the changed verifier prompts end-to-end against the live LLM after the plan-v3 cutover. All ACs prove offline/mocked artifacts (Pydantic rejection, unit-test scoring math) but none runs the actual verifier pipeline with the new prompts to confirm the live model produces grades from {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} in practice. Because the IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bump makes plan-v3 the default for all new verifications (no opt-in flag), E6(f) applies: a cutover to a new path requires at least one AC that exercises it end-to-end as it runs in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('both verifier prompts define each grade with one worked example') and AC2 (impact scoring unit-test pins)
scenarios: The prompt update ships but the LLM still emits legacy grades ('low', 'medium') in practice; Pydantic rejects them (the failure mode is silently caught) and operators see all new verifications erroring at parse time rather than scoring — not caught until production traffic.

A prompt phrasing issue causes the LLM to emit 'underspecified' (dropping '_oracle') for 56 % of findings; unit tests all pass, but live verifications are rejected and fall back to default 0.0 scoring, silently neutering the feature.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'An integration smoke-test (tests/integration/test_plan_verifier_grades.py) runs plan_review_verifier against a seeded plan fixture via the live (or stubbed-at-HTTP-boundary) model and asserts: (a) the returned ac_unverifiable value is in {"none", "underspecified_oracle", "broken_oracle", "missing_oracle"}, and (b) a fixture graded underspecified_oracle scores <= 0.55 through the full pipeline.' This exercises the prompt→parse→score path without requiring a human-in-the-loop attestation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific worked examples to embed in the verifier prompts (one per grade) in the plan or Scope section, rather than leaving the selection to the executor; at minimum, state the selection criterion (e.g. 'the single item nearest the centroid for each grade cluster in the calibration-3 classified sample').
evidence: 'one worked example each drawn from the calibration-3 classified sample' — sample has 100 items; no selection criterion or specific item is named.
The only pointer is 'evidence comment on this story, 2026-07-15' — the executor must locate and read an external comment, then independently choose which of the 100 items best represents each grade.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the executor to include 'one worked example each drawn from the calibration-3 classified sample' in both verifier prompts but does not identify which specific examples from the 100-item classified sample should be used. The executor must choose three examples (one per grade) without guidance, creating a defaultable gap that could produce inconsistent or suboptimal prompt examples across implementations.
findings.location: What section — 'BOTH verifier prompts define the three grades with one worked example each drawn from the calibration-3 classified sample'; Scope — 'plan_review_verifier.md AND plan_review_verifier_agentic.md: three-grade definitions + one worked example each'
scenarios: Two implementors choosing different examples produce prompts whose worked examples are inconsistent or edge-case-y, shifting LLM behavior unpredictably toward one grade.
The calibration-3 comment is not easily navigable; the executor picks arbitrary examples that don't clearly differentiate broken_oracle from underspecified_oracle, undermining prompt quality.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or amend ADR 0036) capturing the oracle-kind three-grade taxonomy: the grade definitions, the UNDERSPECIFIED_ORACLE_CONTRIB invariant and its relationship to blocking thresholds, and the asymmetric attestation rule for broken_oracle — and reference it from docs/plan-review-gate.md.
evidence: Plan names only 'docs/plan-review-gate.md: the floor description updated to the graded rule' as the doc artifact — no ADR creation or ADR 0036 amendment is listed in Scope or ACs.

The asymmetric attestation rule (clears missing/underspecified, never broken_oracle) and the UNDERSPECIFIED_ORACLE_CONTRIB < min(block_threshold) invariant are novel architectural contracts not captured in any named ADR.
ADR 0036 is referenced only for segmentation/back-compat, not as the destination for the new grading semantics.
If a future calibration adds a fourth grade or changes the floor semantics, there is no design-decision record to consult — only the code and a user-facing gate doc.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — the three-grade oracle-kind taxonomy with asymmetric scoring (broken_oracle keeps the floor, underspecified_oracle is scored below blocking) and asymmetric attestation behavior — but only adds a user-facing doc update (docs/plan-review-gate.md) with no ADR or ADR-0036 amendment capturing the design rationale, the grade taxonomy contract, and the UNDERSPECIFIED_ORACLE_CONTRIB invariant for future maintainers.
findings.location: Scope: docs/plan-review-gate.md; What section (oracle-kind grade definitions and scoring mechanics); AC line 1 (both verifier prompts); AC line 3 (IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bump)
scenarios: A future engineer encounters the asymmetric broken_oracle attestation behavior and cannot find the rationale without archaeological code reading.
Calibration 4 introduces a parallel change to another axis; without an ADR the two decisions are not cross-referenced and the emergent scoring model becomes hard to reason about holistically.
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/0037-oracle-kind-ac-unverifiable-grades.md (or amend ADR 0036 with a new section) that records: (1) the three grades and their rationale from calibration-3 data, (2) UNDERSPECIFIED_ORACLE_CONTRIB < min(blocking threshold) as a named invariant, (3) the broken_oracle-never-cleared attestation rule and its justification, and (4) how this composes with the plan-v3 version bump. Add a reference to this ADR in docs/plan-review-gate.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the BACK-COMPAT note: the production sidecar readers (latest_review_result / all_review_results) do NOT segment by impact_model_version — they return any schema='plan_review_result_v2' record including plan-v3 ones; the impact_model_version segmentation is strictly an offline calibration concern, and the plan's back-compat test should pin that a plan-v3 sidecar is correctly returned as the 'latest' on a ticket that has both v2 and v3 records (not just that a v2 fixture round-trips in isolation).
evidence: Plan states: 'legacy plan-v2 sidecars keep their legacy {low,medium,high} grades and are read as-is — every calibration/replay consumer segments by impact_model_version (ADR 0036), so old records are never scored with new semantics'
actual reader code: `if isinstance(payload, dict) and payload.get('schema') in ('plan_review_result_v1', 'plan_review_result_v2')` — no impact_model_version guard anywhere in the reader
plan-v3 records will emit schema='plan_review_result_v2' (plan explicitly states the schema id stays unchanged), so the reader WILL return them to novelty sub-calls
The 'segmentation' mechanism is in calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py (offline), not in sidecar.latest_review_result (production)
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'legacy plan-v2 records are never scored with new semantics' via 'segmentation by impact_model_version (ADR 0036)' as the back-compat mechanism, but the actual production sidecar readers (latest_review_result and all_review_results) have NO impact_model_version gate — they gate only on the schema string. Since plan-v3 sidecars will still emit schema='plan_review_result_v2', ALL plan-v3 records will be returned to any caller of latest_review_result, including the novelty sub-call, immediately after deployment. The segmentation-by-version claim describes offline calibration tooling only, not the production reader path.
findings.location: Scope / BACK-COMPAT section; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py latest_review_result() lines 149-153, all_review_results() lines 198-201
scenarios: After deployment, a ticket's NEWEST sidecar is plan-v3 (with ac_unverifiable='underspecified_oracle'). latest_review_result returns it to the novelty sub-call. The novelty sub-call only reads text/criteria/priority fields so it's safe in practice, but the plan's 'segmentation is the mechanism' assertion is misleading and the stated back-compat test (plan-v2 fixture round-trips) doesn't exercise the correct boundary: it should assert that a MIXED-VERSION ticket dir returns the newest plan-v3 record, not the older plan-v2 one — confirming the production reader has no version fence.
findings.suggested_fix: In the BACK-COMPAT plan section, replace 'segmentation by impact_model_version (ADR 0036), so old records are never scored with new semantics' with 'the production sidecar readers have no version gate — they return any schema=plan_review_result_v2 record including plan-v3 ones; impact_model_version segmentation is exclusively in the offline calibration replay, which the plan-v2-round-trip and UNDERSPECIFIED_ORACLE_CONTRIB invariant tests do not exercise.' Extend the back-compat test to verify a mixed-version ticket dir returns the newest (plan-v3) record from latest_review_result.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in the plan (and in a code comment on ORACLE_GRADE01) why axis-specific special-casing of `ac_unverifiable` is preferred over alternatives that preserve the generic `_SEV01` loop for all axes (e.g., two separate axes, or a per-axis configurable map), so calibration-4 authors have an explicit extension policy for the other three hard-override axes.
evidence: Plan: 'ac_unverifiable is REMOVED from both generic loops and special-cased: (1) contribs = [_SEV01.get(...) for a in _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES if a != "ac_unverifiable"] + [ORACLE_GRADE01.get(...)]; (2) has_override = any(_SEV01.get(...) for a in _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES if a != "ac_unverifiable") or attrs.get("ac_unverifiable") in ("broken_oracle", "missing_oracle")'

Plan: 'The other three override axes (dod_uncertifiable, undecomposed, divergent_implementation) keep their ordinal vocabulary and any-non-none floor, untouched in this story (their grade mix is unmeasured; calibration 4 scope)' — implying the same split may be needed for them, but no extension policy is stated

Codebase: _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES and _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES are uniform-loop constructs in decide.py; all seven axes currently flow through _SEV01 with no per-axis special-casing — ORACLE_GRADE01 is the first structural departure
findings.finding: The plan introduces ORACLE_GRADE01 as the first axis-specific scoring map that breaks the universal _SEV01 ordinal-loop pattern used for all seven plan-severity axes (both the contribs list comprehension and the has_override any()). The novelty of this structural departure — special-casing one axis out of two generic loops instead of, e.g., (a) splitting ac_unverifiable into two separate axes each scored by _SEV01, or (b) a configurable per-axis map table that keeps the generic loop for all axes — is not justified in the plan. Without a stated rationale for choosing axis-specific special-casing over the loop-preserving alternatives, the departure from the established pattern may create an unmaintained extension point: the next axis that needs a non-ordinal grade (calibration-4 scope covers the other three hard-override axes) will face the same choice with no documented policy.
findings.location: What section — SCORING MECHANICS paragraph; Scope — src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py
scenarios: A future calibration-4 story needs to split dod_uncertifiable into oracle-kind grades; without a documented extension policy it either re-invents the pattern or adds a second axis-specific special-case, making the generic loops increasingly hollow.
A reviewer unfamiliar with the calibration-3 context sees ORACLE_GRADE01 as the only axis with a non-_SEV01 map and cannot determine whether this is intentional architecture or a one-off hack.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief rationale note — e.g., 'Two-axis split was rejected because it would multiply the verifier output schema fields; a per-axis configurable map was rejected because it requires the map to be versioned alongside the axis vocabulary; axis-specific special-casing is preferred because ORACLE_GRADE01 is self-documenting and the invariant test catches threshold drift.' — in the plan's SCORING MECHANICS paragraph AND as a code comment adjacent to ORACLE_GRADE01.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the plan-review verification output schema accepts exactly {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} for the axis and rejects anything else at write time,' but the write-time enforcement mechanism lives in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py`'s `PlanSeverityAttrs` class — which currently defines `ac_unverifiable: str = Field(default='none', ...)` with no Literal type or closed-set validator. This file is NOT in the plan's Scope. The prompt-level definitions in the two `.md` files cannot enforce schema rejection at write time; only the Pydantic model in `verify.py` can. The plan's assertion about where closed-set enforcement happens is contradicted by the code structure.
findings.location: Scope section / AC1 ('rejects anything else at write time') / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py (absent from Scope)
findings.suggested_fix: In `verify.py`'s `plan_review_verification_model`, change the `ac_unverifiable` field from `str = Field(default='none')` to `Literal['none', 'underspecified_oracle', 'broken_oracle', 'missing_oracle'] = Field(default='none')` (when `strict=True`) or add a `field_validator` that rejects out-of-vocabulary values on plan-v3 writes. Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` to the Scope.

findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` is absent from Scope but owns `PlanSeverityAttrs.ac_unverifiable`, the Pydantic field whose `description` and effective closed-set enforcement (in strict mode, `ConfigDict(extra='forbid')`) must change from `none|low|medium|high` to `{none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle}`. AC1 requires the schema to accept exactly the new grade set and reject out-of-vocabulary values at write time — that enforcement lives in this file, not in the prompt texts or `decide.py`.
findings.location: Scope section; `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` lines 259-263 (`ac_unverifiable` Field definition in `PlanSeverityAttrs`)
findings.suggested_fix: In `verify.py`'s `PlanSeverityAttrs`, change the `ac_unverifiable` field to: `ac_unverifiable: str = Field(default='none', description='none|underspecified_oracle|broken_oracle|missing_oracle — ...')` and add a `@field_validator('ac_unverifiable')` (or equivalent) that coerces/rejects values outside the new closed set in strict mode.

findings.finding: The plan defines ORACLE_GRADE01 for ac_unverifiable's 'MAX contribution' but does not specify that both internal _SEV01 usages in impact_plan must be replaced for ac_unverifiable: (1) the contribs list (`[_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) for a in _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES]`) and (2) the has_override predicate (`any(_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0 for a in _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES)`). New grade strings like 'broken_oracle' are absent from _SEV01, so both return 0.0 silently — defeating both the impact contribution and the floor trigger for missing_oracle/broken_oracle grades.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for decide.py, add: 'In impact_plan, replace the _SEV01 lookup for ac_unverifiable with ORACLE_GRADE01 in both (a) the contribs list comprehension over _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES and (b) the has_override any() predicate over _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES; the other three override axes keep _SEV01 unchanged.' Also add a test: `assert impact_plan({'ac_unverifiable': 'broken_oracle'}) == 0.85` and `assert impact_plan({'ac_unverifiable': 'underspecified_oracle'}) == 0.55`.

findings.finding: `decide.impact_plan`'s hard-override detection (`has_override`) uses `_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0`, where `_SEV01` is keyed on `{none, low, medium, high}`. The new grade values (`missing_oracle`, `broken_oracle`, `underspecified_oracle`) are NOT in `_SEV01`, so they all resolve to `0.0` — meaning the floor will NEVER fire for any of the new grades, silently defeating the core promise of the change.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or supplement `_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0` with a set-membership check against the new grade vocabulary, e.g. `attrs.get(a) in {"missing_oracle", "broken_oracle"}` for floor-triggering grades and `attrs.get(a) == "underspecified_oracle"` for the capped contribution path; or define a separate `_ORACLE_GRADE_WEIGHT` mapping alongside `_SEV01`.

findings.finding: The plan replaces ac_unverifiable's grade vocabulary from {none, low, medium, high} to {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle}, but `impact_plan` in decide.py scores ALL seven plan-severity axes via the shared `_SEV01` map keyed on {none, low, medium, high}. The new grade strings are absent from `_SEV01`, so `_SEV01.get('underspecified_oracle', 0.0)` → 0.0, `_SEV01.get('broken_oracle', 0.0)` → 0.0, and `_SEV01.get('missing_oracle', 0.0)` → 0.0. This silently breaks BOTH the MAX-contribution leg (`impact_sev`) and the `has_override` predicate for all three new grades — `broken_oracle` and `missing_oracle` (which must floor at 0.85) would not trigger the floor, and none of the three grades would contribute to `impact_sev`. The plan states the floor logic is 'keyed on the ac_unverifiable grade value' but never specifies how the new string grades are mapped to numeric scores or how the existing `_SEV01`/`_PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES` mechanism is adapted for the new vocabulary.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py; cross-ref decide.py lines 48, 166-173 (_SEV01 / _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES / has_override logic)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet for decide.py: 'Introduce a dedicated `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_GRADE` lookup (e.g. `{"none": 0.0, "underspecified_oracle": 0.50, "broken_oracle": 1.0, "missing_oracle": 1.0}`) and update both the MAX-contribution leg and the `has_override` predicate to use it for `ac_unverifiable` specifically, leaving the `_SEV01` path unchanged for the other six axes. The `underspecified_oracle` numeric contribution (e.g. 0.50) must satisfy `min(0.50 * mult, ...) <= 0.55` under all amplifier settings so it can never reach the floor.'

findings.finding: The new oracle-kind grades (underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle) are not in the existing `_SEV01` map in decide.py, yet `impact_plan` uses `_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0)` to both compute the MAX contribution and detect the hard-override predicate. The plan specifies behavioral outcomes (missing/broken floor at 0.85; underspecified scores <=0.55) but never defines the numeric mapping or dispatch mechanism that achieves this — no new lookup table, no conditional branch, no sentinel value. An executor cannot derive the implementation without inventing the protocol.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope entry for decide.py: 'Replace the `_SEV01` lookup for `ac_unverifiable` with a dedicated `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_IMPACT` map: `{"missing_oracle": 1.0, "broken_oracle": 1.0, "underspecified_oracle": 0.55, "none": 0.0}`; the hard-override predicate checks `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_IMPACT.get(attrs.get("ac_unverifiable"), 0.0) >= 0.85` (i.e., only missing/broken trigger it); `underspecified_oracle` contributes 0.55 to the MAX but never triggers the floor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure-handling path when Pydantic rejects an invalid ac_unverifiable grade (retry, hard error with surfaced message, or safe fallback), and add at least one instrumented signal (log warning + metric counter) so operators can detect and diagnose the validation failure rate.
evidence: 'its ac_unverifiable field changes from the ordinal type to Literal["none", "underspecified_oracle", "broken_oracle", "missing_oracle"], so Pydantic strict validation rejects out-of-vocabulary grades at verification-parse time'
ABSENCE: no retry/fallback/error-surfacing behavior is described for the rejection path
ABSENCE: no log/metric/alert instrumentation is specified for the new validation failure point
findings.finding: When Pydantic strict validation rejects an out-of-vocabulary ac_unverifiable grade from the LLM (e.g., the model emits 'low' or 'high' for a new plan-v3 verification), the plan specifies the rejection but does not specify the failure-handling path: does the calling workflow retry, raise a hard error that aborts the review, fall back to a safe default, or surface a user-visible error? An unhandled parse failure here could silently drop a review result or crash the pipeline.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py; What section: 'Pydantic strict validation rejects out-of-vocabulary grades at verification-parse time'
scenarios: LLM emits a legacy grade string ('low', 'medium') in a plan-v3 context (e.g., prompt regression, model version change) — Pydantic rejects it, but the plan says nothing about what happens next; the review may silently fail or crash.
A prompt template bug causes malformed output — without a specified error path, operators have no instrumented signal to detect the failure rate spike.
findings.suggested_fix: In verify.py, wrap the PlanSeverityAttrs parse in a try/except ValidationError; on failure, log a structured warning with the raw grade value and increment a `plan_review.ac_unverifiable_parse_rejection` counter, then either retry the LLM call (if retryable) or raise a typed exception that the calling workflow surfaces as a review-error state rather than a silent drop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for Pydantic validation failure on ac_unverifiable (e.g., catch ValidationError → degrade to INDETERMINATE with a logged contract violation, or retry once with explicit grade instruction) so the executor knows how to handle the transitional period.
evidence: Plan quote: 'Pydantic strict validation rejects out-of-vocabulary grades at verification-parse time — that field is the closed-set gate the ACs pin.'
Plan quote: 'An unknown/legacy grade string maps to 0.0 via .get() default in plan-v3 scoring' — this handles the scoring path but not the parse-rejection path.

The existing code in workflow_ops.py lines 418-424 shows a reshape/contract-violation handler that degrades to INDETERMINATE — but it is not stated whether a Pydantic Literal validation failure is caught and routed here, or propagates as an unhandled exception.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the fallback behavior when Pydantic strict validation REJECTS an out-of-vocabulary ac_unverifiable grade from the LLM during a new plan-v3 verification (e.g., the model emits 'low' or 'high' despite the prompt update). The plan says 'Pydantic strict validation rejects out-of-vocabulary grades at verification-parse time' but is silent on what happens next: retry the verification pass, degrade the finding to INDETERMINATE, treat the field as 'none', or raise an unhandled error.
findings.location: What section: 'WRITE-TIME ENFORCEMENT' paragraph; Scope: verify.py item
scenarios: If the LLM emits 'low' for ac_unverifiable on a plan-v3 prompt (a likely regression, since the prompt text update might take time to propagate), Pydantic raises a ValidationError; if this is not caught, the verification pass fails entirely, blocking the review gate from completing rather than gracefully degrading.

Different callers (strict=True vs strict=False) of plan_review_verification_model already exist; the rejection path only fires for strict=True callers — the plan does not clarify which mode the live gate uses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py` and the `enrich_operator_attested` test in `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py` to Scope, and specify that their fixtures using legacy grade strings (`'high'`) must be updated to use the new oracle-kind vocabulary (e.g., `'broken_oracle'` or `'missing_oracle'`).
evidence: test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py:48: verifs = {0: {"severity_attributes": {"ac_unverifiable": "high"}, "binary": {}}} — guard expects this to be cleared
test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py:84: assert verifs[0]["severity_attributes"].get("ac_unverifiable") == "none" — clearing asserted
test_plan_review.py:369: verifs = {0: {"severity_attributes": {"ac_unverifiable": "high"}, "binary": {}}}; line 374: assert attrs.get("ac_unverifiable") == "none"

workflow_ops.py:115: current guard `if attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none'):` will become `in ('missing_oracle', 'underspecified_oracle')` per plan — 'high' will NOT match the new guard
Neither file appears in Scope or in the unit-test bullet of the What section
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py and tests/unit/test_plan_review.py (test_enrich_operator_attested_clears_ac_unverifiable_upstream) are unenumerated consumers of enrich_operator_attested that will break when the guard changes. Both files set ac_unverifiable='high' and assert it is cleared — after the guard changes from `not in (None, '', 'none')` to `in ('missing_oracle', 'underspecified_oracle')`, the string 'high' will no longer match the guard condition, so the clear will not fire and the tests will fail.
scenarios: After the guard change, a finding with ac_unverifiable='high' (legacy or operator-attested edge case) no longer gets cleared — the held-out edge tests will fail with AssertionError.
The CI gate fails on existing test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py and test_plan_review.py after the scope changes land.
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope's unit-test bullet, add: 'tests/unit/test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py: update fixture grades from legacy {low/medium/high} to oracle-kind grades; tests/unit/test_plan_review.py: update test_enrich_operator_attested_clears_ac_unverifiable_upstream fixture to use missing_oracle/underspecified_oracle in the verif dict.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
